skill.name: docx
skill.description: Create, inspect, and edit Microsoft Word .docx files with OOXML workflows, tracked changes, comments, validation, and document conversion guidance.
skill.summary: Work with Word .docx files through OOXML editing, tracked changes, comments, and validation workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: The skill has legitimate DOCX and OOXML document-processing intent, and many static alerts are false positives from markdown code blocks, XML namespaces, and OOXML vocabulary. However, several bundled scripts extract user-supplied Office archives with extractall() and no member path validation, which is a confirmed Zip Slip risk. Publication should wait until archive extraction is hardened and privileged install guidance is removed or clearly isolated.

high_findings.title: Unsafe archive extraction of Office documents
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The unpacking and validation helpers call ZipFile.extractall() on Office files without validating member paths. A crafted DOCX/PPTX/XLSX archive could write files outside the intended temporary or output directory.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses extractall() on user-provided Office archives. I found no path normalization or member rejection before extraction at these call sites.

medium_findings.title: External command execution in document workflows
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with constrained scope: The skill instructs agents to run pandoc, soffice, pdftoppm, and bundled scripts, and pack.py invokes soffice through subprocess.run(). The subprocess calls use argument arrays rather than shell=True, so command injection evidence is not present, but untrusted documents are processed by external binaries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command paths are explicit and semantically part of the workflow. The risk is reduced because subprocess arguments are not passed through a shell.

medium_findings.title: Privileged dependency installation guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as marketplace risk: The documentation tells users or agents to install system packages with sudo apt-get. This is normal setup guidance, but marketplace skills should avoid routine privileged commands in executable workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands are visible in dependency instructions. They are not hidden or malicious, but they can expand execution privileges if followed automatically.

medium_findings.title: Broad filesystem modification by document helper library
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE with legitimate purpose: The Document helper copies input directories to temporary workspaces, writes XML files, and copies results back to the destination. This is expected for DOCX editing but should be limited to user-approved paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem operations are clear and central to the skill. I did not find evidence of stealthy persistence or unauthorized target paths in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are mostly namespaces or examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The network alerts mostly point to license URLs, OOXML namespace URIs, relationship type URIs, and a sample hyperlink. I did not find evidence that the skill sends data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs appear in documentation, licenses, or XML relationship examples. No HTTP client or exfiltration flow was found in those locations.

low_findings.title: OOXML terms triggered weak crypto and C2 keyword alerts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static matches such as RSID, id attributes, schema names, and XML vocabulary appear to be document-format terminology rather than cryptographic implementation or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed examples are OOXML metadata and identifier handling. No malicious C2 control flow or weak cryptographic security decision was found in these snippets.

low_findings.title: Long XML template lines resemble obfuscation
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The long template files are Word comment XML templates with many namespace declarations. This format explains the line length without requiring obfuscated executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static signal is explained by dense XML templates. I did not find JavaScript obfuscation or runtime decoding behavior in the cited template files.

dangerous_patterns.title: zipfile.extractall without path validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill extracts archive members directly into output directories. Replace this with safe extraction that rejects absolute paths and paths resolving outside the destination.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This is a direct dangerous API pattern on archive input. The reviewed call sites do not perform member path checks first.

dangerous_patterns.title: Agent-facing sudo install commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes sudo apt-get dependency commands in instructions. This should be separated from normal skill execution and require explicit user approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented plainly. The concern is operational privilege expansion, not hidden malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Word document automation often requires direct OOXML work that is hard to perform by hand. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows for creating, editing, redlining, validating, and converting .docx files.
actual_capabilities: Creates new .docx files using the documented docx-js workflow.
Unpacks Office Open XML archives for direct XML inspection and editing.
Adds comments, replies, insertions, and deletions through a Python Document helper.
Preserves tracked-change patterns for legal, business, academic, and review documents.
Converts documents to markdown, PDF, and page images when required tools are installed.
Validates edited documents against OOXML and redlining rules before repacking.
limitations: Requires local tools such as pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, Node.js, and Python packages for full use.
Does not claim support for encrypted or password-protected Word files.
Complex layouts may still require manual OOXML inspection and repair.
Current archive extraction helpers need hardening before publication for untrusted documents.

use_cases.title: Review contracts with tracked changes
use_cases.description: Apply precise insertions, deletions, and comments while keeping Word revision history readable.

use_cases.title: Prepare technical reports
use_cases.description: Create structured Word documents with headings, tables, images, citations, and validation checks.

use_cases.title: Extract and inspect document content
use_cases.description: Convert DOCX files to markdown or inspect raw XML to audit formatting, comments, and embedded media.

prompt_templates.title: Extract document text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the docx workflow to extract readable text from this Word document and summarize its sections.

prompt_templates.title: Create a report
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a .docx report with a title page, three section headings, a table, and a short conclusion.

prompt_templates.title: Add review comments
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this .docx file and add Word comments to unclear clauses without changing the original text.

prompt_templates.title: Apply redlined edits
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply these requested revisions as tracked changes, validate the OOXML, and produce a reviewed .docx file.

output_examples.input: A contract needs five clause updates and reviewer comments.
output: A revised Word document with tracked insertions and deletions.
Reviewer comments attached to the relevant clauses.
A short validation summary for the edited document.

output_examples.input: A technical outline needs to become a formatted Word report.
output: A generated .docx file with headings, lists, tables, and image placeholders.
Consistent Word styles for title, headings, body text, and captions.

output_examples.input: A DOCX file needs content inspection before migration.
output: Notes about comments, tracked changes, media files, and document structure.

best_practices: Use tracked changes for documents owned by another person or organization.
Validate OOXML after each meaningful batch of edits.
Work only on copies of important documents until the final file is verified.
anti_patterns: Do not run archive extraction on untrusted files without safe path checks.
Do not edit another author's tracked-change XML directly.
Do not skip validation after manual XML edits.

faq.question: Can this skill create new Word documents?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a docx-js workflow for creating .docx files with sections, paragraphs, tables, lists, links, and images.

faq.question: Can it edit existing DOCX files?
faq.answer: Yes. It unpacks the OOXML archive, edits XML through Python helpers, validates the result, and repacks the document.

faq.question: Does it support tracked changes?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance emphasizes precise insertions, deletions, comments, and redlining validation for reviewed documents.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: Full workflows may require pandoc, LibreOffice, Poppler, Node.js with docx, Python, and defusedxml.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted documents?
faq.answer: Not without fixes. The current archive extraction helpers should validate ZIP member paths before processing untrusted files.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence found in reviewed code. Static URL alerts mostly reference licenses, XML namespaces, and documentation examples.
